items.text: Your real edge over the pros isn't brains — it's freedom. A fund manager reports every three months and loses clients (and their job) if they trail the market for a year or two, even when they're ultimately right. So they cling to safe, index-like bets. You answer to no one. You can hold a good idea through three ugly years — the exact move that would get a professional fired. That patience is the edge.

Between 1999 and 2001 Amazon's stock fell 90% — while the business was actually getting stronger: more warehouses, more customers, better economics. Price and true worth moved in opposite directions for two whole years. The lesson: price can be a wildly misleading signal of how a business is really doing, for long stretches. Long-term investors buy the business, not the ticker.

Compounding rewards patience unfairly. ₹1 growing 15% a year becomes ₹16 over 20 years; at 25% it becomes ₹86. Same 20 years, five times the money — because each year's growth builds on the last. This is why a steady, durable return matters far more than one dazzling year. (The shorthand for that annual rate is CAGR: the smooth yearly pace that gets you from start to finish.)
Why can't most fund managers simply choose a 10-year view, even when they agree it works?
options: Regulations forbid holding stocks that long
Quarterly reporting + client redemptions make interim underperformance career-fatal
Long-term data isn't available to them
items.explanation: It's structural, not intellectual: clients redeem during drawdowns, so managers who lag for two years lose assets and jobs — even if ultimately right.

items.text: During 1999–2001 Amazon's stock fell 90%. What was its intrinsic value most plausibly doing?
options: Falling roughly in line with the stock
Rising — infrastructure, customers and unit economics were improving
Flat — nothing changes in two years
items.explanation: The business compounded value while sentiment collapsed. Price and value can diverge for years — the long-horizon investor's opportunity.

items.text: A stock goes from ₹200 to ₹520 in 10 years. Roughly what CAGR is that?
items.explanation: CAGR = (520/200)^(0.1) − 1 = 2.6^0.1 − 1 ≈ 10%. Dividing total return by years (option A) ignores compounding and overstates the rate.

items.text: On screener.in, open Pidilite. Find its 10-year Profit growth (CAGR) and its 10-year stock-price CAGR — both are shown on the page. Are they roughly similar? Over long periods a stock's return tends to track how fast the underlying profits grew — proof that owning the business, not trading the price, is what pays. Note the two numbers and how close they are. (~5 min)
